_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan (English I) style reading, batch F3. ~300-360 word abstract argumentative passages, 4 MCQs each (attitude/inference/main idea/argument/vocabulary). DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: Few flattering self-images are as durable as that of the self-made man, who attributes his success entirely to his own talent and toil and owes, as he sees it, nothing to anyone. The image is appealing because it is, in part, deserved; effort and ability are real, and to deny their contribution would be both false and demoralising. But the picture is radically incomplete, and its incompleteness is not innocent. For no one's success is the product of his exertions alone. It depends, invariably, on a vast inheritance of circumstance: on being born in a prosperous rather than a wretched place and time, on the accident of a particular talent's happening to be valued by one's society, on the countless supports — of family, education, infrastructure, and sheer good fortune — without which the same effort would have come to nothing. The self-made man has merely forgotten, or never noticed, how much was given to him before he began. Why does this forgetting matter? Because the belief that one's advantages are wholly earned licenses a hardness towards those who lack them. If I owe my position entirely to my own merit, then those below me must owe their condition entirely to their own deficiency, and I am absolved of any obligation towards them. The myth of the self-made man is thus not a harmless vanity but the foundation of a comfortable indifference, a way of converting good luck into supposed desert and so escaping the claims that fortune's arbitrariness might otherwise press upon the fortunate. To recognise the role of luck in one's success is not to belittle one's efforts; it is to acquire the humility, and the sense of obligation, that a truer account of how anyone arrives anywhere ought to inspire. To acknowledge the luck in our successes is not a counsel of false modesty but the beginning of a juster vision, replacing the comfortable fiction that the prosperous have earned all they have, and the poor deserve all they lack, with the harder truth that we are all, in large measure, the beneficiaries or victims of circumstances we did not choose.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer call the self-made image 'radically incomplete'?
options: Because effort plays no role at all.
Because success always depends on a vast inheritance of circumstance and luck.

questions.prompt: What belief, the writer says, 'licenses a hardness' towards others?
options: That one's advantages are wholly earned by merit.

questions.prompt: What does the myth of the self-made man become, in the writer's view?
options: The foundation of a comfortable indifference towards the less fortunate.

questions.prompt: What does recognising the role of luck inspire, according to the writer?
options: Humility and a sense of obligation.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 成功总依赖境遇与运气的庞大遗产)、细节(B '优势全凭自挣'之信念纵容对他人的冷硬)、细节(B 此神话成为对不幸者安然冷漠之基)、主旨/态度(B 承认运气带来谦逊与责任感)。

sets.passage: Boredom has so poor a reputation that we organise much of modern life around its avoidance, and the means of avoidance are now so abundant that genuine boredom has become, for many, a rare and quickly banished experience. At the first stirring of tedium, a screen offers instant relief, and the empty interval that might once have had to be endured is filled before it can deepen. This seems an unambiguous gain. Yet there are reasons to suspect that in abolishing boredom we may be losing something we did not know we needed. Boredom, for all its discomfort, is not merely an absence to be filled. It is, on closer inspection, a signal and a spur. The restlessness it induces is the mind's protest against insufficient stimulation, and that protest has traditionally driven us to seek stimulation of a particular kind — to read, to think, to make, to imagine, to undertake the demanding activities through which boredom is not merely relieved but transcended. The bored child, denied a screen, is thrown back upon his own resources, and in the effort to entertain himself discovers the rudiments of creativity and self-reliance. When boredom can always be dissolved by a passive and effortless distraction, this productive pressure is removed. The vacancy is filled, but filled with nothing that nourishes, and the capacities that boredom once goaded into action are left undeveloped. The paradox is that the very ease with which we now escape boredom may impoverish us, depriving us of the fertile discomfort from which, historically, much of human thought and art has sprung. To be never bored is not, it turns out, an unmixed blessing, but may be a kind of starvation disguised as plenty. To recover the uses of boredom is not to court tedium for its own sake but to resist the reflex that fills every empty moment before it can do its work; the mind, like a field, may need its fallow intervals, and a life from which all such intervals have been banished may prove, for all its stimulation, strangely barren.

questions.prompt: How do most people now treat boredom, according to the writer?
options: They organise life around avoiding it, banishing it instantly.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say boredom actually is, on closer inspection?
options: A mere absence to be filled.
A signal and a spur that drives us to demanding activity.

questions.prompt: What happens when boredom can always be dissolved by passive distraction?
options: The productive pressure is removed and certain capacities go undeveloped.

questions.prompt: What is the 'paradox' the writer identifies?
options: That the ease of escaping boredom may impoverish us.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 人们围绕避免无聊安排生活、即时驱散)、细节(B 无聊其实是信号与刺激、驱人投入费力活动)、细节(B 被动消遣抹去这种生产性压力、能力得不到发展)、主旨(B 逃避无聊之易反而使人贫乏)。

sets.passage: The impulse to measure has become one of the governing forces of contemporary institutions, and its appeal is not hard to understand. Numbers promise objectivity; they permit comparison, ranking and accountability; they replace the messy uncertainties of judgement with the clean authority of data. A school, a hospital or a company that can be assessed by precise metrics seems thereby rendered transparent, its performance laid open to scrutiny and improvement. Yet the very power of measurement conceals a characteristic danger, one that recurs wherever metrics are made to bear too much weight. The danger is this: that what can be measured is rarely identical with what matters, and that once a measure becomes a target, it ceases even to measure well. When a quantity that approximates some valued but elusive quality is made the object of reward and punishment, those subject to it learn, rationally, to maximise the quantity rather than the quality it was meant to track. The teacher teaches to the test; the hospital, judged by waiting times, finds ways to reclassify the waiting; the researcher, rewarded for publications, produces many thin papers in place of few substantial ones. In each case the metric is satisfied while the purpose it was meant to serve is subverted, and the institution, congratulating itself on its measured success, drifts further from the goal it cannot measure. The lesson is not that measurement is worthless — it can discipline vague impressions and expose real failures — but that it is a servant which makes a tyrannical master. Used to inform judgement, it is invaluable; used to replace judgement, it corrupts the very activities it was meant to improve, rewarding the appearance of the good at the expense of its substance. The wisdom required is to keep measurement firmly in its place, as one input among many into a judgement that no formula can replace; an institution that has forgotten how to value what it cannot count will, in the end, find itself excelling at its metrics while quietly failing at its mission.

questions.prompt: Why does measurement appeal to modern institutions?
options: Because numbers promise objectivity, comparison and accountability.

questions.prompt: What happens 'once a measure becomes a target', according to the writer?
options: It ceases even to measure well, as people maximise the quantity not the quality.

questions.prompt: What do the examples of teacher, hospital and researcher illustrate?
options: That the metric is satisfied while its underlying purpose is subverted.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's overall 'lesson' about measurement?
options: That it is a good servant but a tyrannical master.
That it should replace all judgement.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 数字许诺客观、可比与问责)、细节(B 一旦度量变成目标、人们追求数量而非质量、它便失去衡量之效)、细节(B 例子说明指标被满足而其本意被颠覆)、主旨(B 度量是好仆人却是暴君般的主人)。

sets.passage: We commonly regard forgetting as a defect — a failure of a faculty that ought, ideally, to retain everything — and we dream of technologies that might one day grant us a perfect and total recall. The dream deserves more suspicion than it usually receives, for a memory that forgot nothing would be not a blessing but a curse, and the supposed defect of forgetting performs a function so vital that without it the mind could scarcely work at all. Consider what total recall would mean. Every trivial perception, every momentary irritation, every wound and embarrassment, would persist with undimmed vividness, equal in weight to the experiences that truly mattered. The past would not recede; it would accumulate, an ever-growing burden pressing with full force upon the present, until the sheer mass of undifferentiated detail overwhelmed the capacity to think, to generalise, to act. For thought depends on selection, and selection is impossible without discarding. To form a concept is to ignore the particulars that distinguish one instance from another; to learn from experience is to extract the pattern and let the incidental detail fall away. Forgetting, far from being a malfunction, is the mind's mechanism of selection, the means by which it sifts the significant from the trivial and keeps the past in its proper, diminished place. There is, too, a mercy in it. The healing of grief, the fading of humiliation, the capacity to forgive and to begin again — all depend on the merciful dimming that time, through forgetting, performs. A being condemned to remember everything would be condemned also to nurse every injury forever, incapable of the release that forgetting alone affords. What we lament as a weakness is, rightly seen, among the conditions of a bearable and a functioning life. To wish for total recall is thus to wish for a kind of paralysis, mistaking a mercy for a defect; the mind that forgets is not a flawed version of one that remembers everything but a better-designed one, and the technologies that promise to abolish forgetting may rob us of a faculty as essential to a bearable life as memory itself.

questions.prompt: How do we 'commonly regard' forgetting?
options: As a defect, a failure of a faculty that ought to retain everything.

questions.prompt: What would total recall mean, according to the writer?
options: An ever-growing burden of undifferentiated detail overwhelming thought.

questions.prompt: Why is forgetting essential to thought?
options: Because thought depends on selection, which is impossible without discarding.

questions.prompt: What 'mercy' does forgetting provide?
options: It allows the healing of grief and the capacity to forgive and begin again.
It makes the past more vivid.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 我们把遗忘视为缺陷)、细节(B 全记忆=不断累积的无差别细节压垮思维)、细节(B 思维依赖选择、而选择必须舍弃)、主旨(B 遗忘带来疗愈悲伤、宽恕与重新开始的慈悲)。

sets.passage: Nostalgia, the bittersweet longing for a vanished past, is among the gentlest of human feelings, and it would seem churlish to find fault with so harmless an indulgence. Yet nostalgia, when it hardens from a private sentiment into a public creed, acquires a mischief disproportionate to its tender origins, and deserves a more critical scrutiny than its sweetness usually invites. The trouble begins with the kind of past that nostalgia remembers. It is never the past as it actually was, with its full measure of hardship, injustice and fear, but a past selectively edited and softly lit, from which the miseries have been quietly removed and only the comforts retained. The good old days were good, in memory, largely because memory has performed upon them precisely the work of forgetting that no lived present can enjoy; we compare the remembered past, purged of its pains, with the present felt in all its raw imperfection, and the present cannot but suffer by the contrast. This would be a harmless confusion were it merely personal. But nostalgia readily becomes political, furnishing the conviction that some former age was a golden time from which we have fallen, and that salvation lies in return. Such a conviction is doubly dangerous: it slanders the present, which for all its faults is rarely worse and often better than the idealised past; and it offers, as a programme, the recovery of something that never existed, a restoration of a condition that owes its appeal entirely to the editing of memory. The longing for a better world is wholesome and necessary; but when it disguises itself as a longing for the past, it mistakes a fiction for a destination, and squanders on an imaginary yesterday the energies that a real tomorrow requires. The cure for false nostalgia is not to despise the past but to remember it honestly, with its shadows as well as its light; only a clear-eyed account of where we have come from can free the energies of longing from their backward fixation and turn them, where they belong, towards the unfinished and improvable present.

questions.prompt: What kind of past does nostalgia remember, according to the writer?
options: The past exactly as it was.
A selectively edited past with miseries removed and comforts retained.
A past worse than the present.

questions.prompt: Why does the present 'suffer by the contrast'?
options: Because the present is genuinely worse.
Because we compare a purged past with the present felt in all its raw imperfection.
Because the past had no hardships.

questions.prompt: Why is political nostalgia 'doubly dangerous'?
options: It slanders the present and offers the recovery of something that never existed.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's view of the longing for a better world?
options: It is wholesome, but mistaken when disguised as a longing for the past.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 怀旧记住的是删去苦难、只留温情的过去)、细节(B 拿净化的过去比当下之粗粝、当下必败)、细节(B 政治怀旧诽谤当下、又许诺复原从未存在之物)、主旨/态度(B 向往更好世界是健康的、但伪装成怀旧便错把虚构当目标)。

sets.passage: The art of persuasion is as old as speech, and there is nothing inherently sinister in the attempt to bring others round to one's view; argument, advocacy and the marshalling of evidence are the very lifeblood of reasoned exchange. But a line divides legitimate persuasion from its corrupt cousin, manipulation, and the modern apparatus of advertising and propaganda has grown adept at crossing it while disguising the trespass. The distinction is not always easy to draw, yet it is real and worth the effort. Legitimate persuasion addresses its target as a rational agent, offering reasons that the listener is free to weigh and, if he finds them wanting, to reject. It works through the understanding and respects the autonomy of the one persuaded, who emerges from the exchange having genuinely made up his own mind. Manipulation, by contrast, bypasses the reason it cannot honestly satisfy. It works upon the listener through channels he does not consciously monitor — through the exploitation of fear, vanity and desire, through association and repetition and the artful management of impressions — aiming not to convince him but to move him, while leaving him under the illusion that he has convinced himself. The manipulated man is not persuaded but engineered; his apparent choice is the manipulator's choice, implanted by means that work the better for being unrecognised. What makes this distinction more than academic is that a society saturated with manipulation suffers a peculiar corrosion. Where the techniques of unreason prevail, the very capacity for rational deliberation atrophies, and citizens accustomed to being moved rather than convinced grow incapable of the patient weighing of reasons on which self-government depends. The defence of honest persuasion against its manipulative counterfeit is therefore not merely a matter of personal hygiene but of preserving the conditions under which reasoned collective life remains possible at all. To preserve the distinction between persuading and manipulating is to defend more than good manners in argument; it is to defend the very possibility of a public realm in which free minds reason together towards shared conclusions, rather than a marketplace in which they are merely worked upon by whoever commands the most powerful techniques of influence.

questions.prompt: How does legitimate persuasion treat its target?
options: As an object to be moved.
As a rational agent, offering reasons he is free to weigh or reject.

questions.prompt: How does manipulation work, according to the writer?
options: By bypassing reason, exploiting fear, vanity and desire below conscious notice.

questions.prompt: What is true of the 'manipulated man'?
options: He has genuinely made up his own mind.
His apparent choice is really the manipulator's, implanted unrecognised.
He has weighed the reasons carefully.

questions.prompt: Why is the distinction 'more than academic'?
options: A society saturated with manipulation loses the capacity for rational deliberation.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 正当说服待人为理性主体、提供可自由权衡的理由)、细节(B 操纵绕过理性、利用恐惧虚荣欲望)、细节(B 被操纵者的'选择'实为操纵者所植入)、主旨(B 操纵泛滥之社会丧失理性审议能力)。

sets.passage: The words solitude and loneliness are often used as if they were interchangeable, both denoting the simple condition of being alone, and this conflation obscures a distinction of the first importance. For the two states, though they may share an outward circumstance, are inwardly opposed, and a life may be rich in one while wholly free of the other. Loneliness is the painful sense of an absence — of connection withheld, of a self unmet and unconfirmed by others. It is suffered rather than chosen, and it can afflict a person as acutely in a crowd as in an empty room, for its essence is not physical isolation but a felt estrangement, a hunger for a communion that is lacking. Solitude is something else entirely. It is the chosen and fruitful condition of being alone with oneself, not as a deprivation but as an opportunity — for reflection, for creation, for the recovery of a self that the constant presence of others tends to scatter and submerge. Where loneliness is empty, solitude is full; where loneliness craves escape, solitude is sought and guarded. The confusion of the two is not innocent, for it leads a society fearful of loneliness to flee solitude as well, treating every unaccompanied moment as a danger to be remedied by company or distraction. But to lose the capacity for solitude is to lose access to a region of experience in which much that is most valuable in human life has always been found — the deepest thought, the truest art, the encounter with oneself that mere sociability forever postpones. The remedy for loneliness is connection; but the cultivation of solitude is a different and equally necessary art, and a culture that cannot tell them apart impoverishes itself in the very act of seeking comfort. To recover the art of solitude is not to retreat from human connection but to make oneself fit for it; the person who cannot bear to be alone brings to every relationship a neediness no companionship can satisfy, while one who has learned to be at home in their own company has a self worth offering to another.

questions.prompt: What is loneliness, according to the writer?
options: The chosen condition of being alone with oneself.
The painful sense of an absence of connection, suffered rather than chosen.

questions.prompt: How does the writer characterise solitude?
options: As the chosen and fruitful condition of being alone with oneself.
As a danger to be remedied.

questions.prompt: Why is the confusion of the two 'not innocent'?
options: It leads a society fearful of loneliness to flee solitude as well.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say is lost when the capacity for solitude is lost?
options: Access to the deepest thought, truest art and the encounter with oneself.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 孤独是连接缺失之痛、被动承受)、细节(B 独处是自择而丰盈的与己独处)、细节(B 二者混淆使惧怕孤独的社会也逃避独处)、主旨(B 失去独处之能即失去最深思想、真艺术与自我相遇之途)。

sets.passage: Among the deepest transformations wrought by the modern age is one that is easy to overlook precisely because it is so complete: what an earlier thinker called the disenchantment of the world. For most of human history, the world was experienced as saturated with meaning and presence — animated by spirits, ordered by providence, charged at every point with purposes that addressed themselves to humanity. The river, the storm, the harvest were not mere mechanisms but the doings of powers with whom one stood in relation, and the cosmos as a whole was a meaningful order in which human life had an assigned and intelligible place. The rise of science has dissolved this enchanted vision with extraordinary thoroughness. The world it reveals is one of matter in motion, governed by impersonal laws that pursue no purpose and regard humanity not at all; the river obeys hydraulics, the storm thermodynamics, and nowhere in the whole vast machinery is there any trace of the meaning that earlier ages found everywhere. This is, in the order of knowledge, an immense gain, and there is no honest going back. But it has left a wound that the gain does not heal. Human beings seem constituted to crave a meaning that the disenchanted world withholds, to ask of their existence a significance that a universe of blind mechanism cannot supply. The characteristic restlessness of modern life — its hunger for purposes, its susceptibility to substitutes for the faith it has lost — may be understood as the symptom of this unhealed wound, the predicament of a creature that has learned, irreversibly, that the cosmos is silent, yet cannot stop addressing to it the question of why. Disenchantment, in this light, is not merely an episode in the history of ideas but the defining spiritual situation of the age. To live honestly in a disenchanted world is neither to pretend the old enchantment back into being nor to deny the hunger for meaning that science cannot feed, but to inhabit the tension knowingly — accepting the silence of the cosmos while continuing, undeceived, to make what meaning we can within it, which may be the most an age such as ours can honestly ask of itself.

questions.prompt: How was the world experienced 'for most of human history'?
options: As saturated with meaning, animated by spirits and ordered by providence.

questions.prompt: What world does the rise of science reveal?
options: An enchanted cosmos full of purpose.
Matter governed by impersonal laws that pursue no purpose.
A world that regards humanity highly.

questions.prompt: What 'wound' does disenchantment leave?
options: Humans crave a meaning that the disenchanted world withholds.

questions.prompt: How does the writer interpret modern restlessness?
options: As a symptom of the unhealed wound left by disenchantment.
As proof the cosmos has meaning.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 历史上世界被体验为充满意义、有神灵与天意)、细节(B 科学揭示的是无目的的非人格规律支配之物质)、细节(B 祛魅留下创伤:人渴求世界不予的意义)、主旨(B 现代不安是这未愈创伤之症候)。

sets.passage: Hope is so universally praised that to speak against it seems almost a kind of cruelty, and indeed the case for hope is strong: it sustains effort, consoles suffering, and supplies the forward momentum without which few hard things would ever be attempted. Yet hope has a shadow side, less often acknowledged, and a clear-eyed account must reckon with the harm that hope, misplaced, can do. The first danger is that hope may become a substitute for action rather than a spur to it. The person who hopes that things will improve may, on the strength of that hope, neglect to do what improvement actually requires, soothed by an expectation that excuses him from exertion. Hope, in this form, is a narcotic, dulling the discomfort that might otherwise have driven him to act. The second danger lies in hope's relation to truth. To hope for an outcome is to wish it, and the wish exerts a quiet pressure on the judgement, inclining us to believe what we should like to be so and to discount the evidence that it is not. Hope thus tends to corrupt the realism on which effective action depends, leading us to persist in doomed courses long after a colder eye would have abandoned them. None of this is an argument against hope as such, which remains, on balance, a friend to human striving. It is an argument for a disciplined hope — one held together with, rather than against, a sober assessment of the facts; one that motivates without deceiving, and that knows the difference between the determination to bring about a possible good and the comfortable fantasy that the good will arrive of its own accord. The hope worth having is not the enemy of clear sight but its ally, and it is distinguished from its dangerous counterfeits precisely by its refusal to purchase comfort at the price of truth. To cultivate such a hope is among the harder disciplines of a mature mind, for it asks us to hold steady against both the despair that gives up too soon and the wishful thinking that will not look at unwelcome facts, sustaining the effort that change requires without the comforting lie that change is assured.

questions.prompt: What is the 'first danger' of hope the writer identifies?
options: That it may become a substitute for action, a narcotic dulling discomfort.
That it is impossible to feel.

questions.prompt: How does hope relate to truth, in its second danger?
options: It inclines us to believe what we wish and discount contrary evidence.
It has no effect on judgement.

questions.prompt: What kind of hope does the writer advocate?
options: A disciplined hope held together with a sober assessment of the facts.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes 'the hope worth having' from its counterfeits?
options: Its refusal to purchase comfort at the price of truth.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 第一危险:希望成为行动的替代品、麻醉不适)、细节(B 第二危险:倾向相信所愿、贬抑反证、腐蚀现实感)、细节(B 主张有纪律的希望、与冷静评估facts并行)、主旨/态度(B 值得拥有的希望拒绝以真相换取安慰)。

sets.passage: Empathy — the capacity to feel what another feels, to enter imaginatively into a life not one's own — is widely held to be the foundation of morality, and its cultivation is urged as the remedy for cruelty and indifference. There is much in this, and a world with more empathy would in many ways be a better one. But empathy, examined closely, proves a less reliable moral guide than its champions suppose, and an ethics that rested on it alone would be gravely defective. The trouble is that empathy is, by its nature, partial and innumerate. We feel for the individual before us, vividly and at once; we cannot feel, with anything like the same force, for the thousands or millions we cannot see. The single identifiable victim moves us to action that the statistical multitude, however vastly greater their suffering, cannot. Empathy thus systematically distorts moral attention, directing our concern towards the near, the visible and the few, and away from the distant, the abstract and the many, in defiance of any rational accounting of where help is most needed. Worse, empathy is easily enlisted by manipulation: the vivid case, skilfully presented, can mobilise feeling on behalf of a cause that sober judgement would reject, and the very intensity of fellow-feeling can override the considerations that ought to temper it. None of this is to disparage empathy, which remains an indispensable spur to concern. It is to insist that empathy must be governed by something cooler and more impartial — by principles, by reasoning, by a deliberate widening of the circle that feeling alone will never accomplish. Compassion that is not disciplined by justice is not a sufficient morality but a partial and manipulable sentiment, and to mistake the warmth of fellow-feeling for the whole of ethics is to build on a foundation that will not bear the weight. To temper empathy with justice is not to grow colder but to become more genuinely good; the warmth of fellow-feeling remains the indispensable spark, but only when that warmth is guided by impartial reason does it reliably reach those who most need it, rather than merely those whose faces we happen to see.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer call empathy 'partial and innumerate'?
options: Because it feels equally for everyone.
Because we feel for the visible individual but not for the unseen multitude.
Because it never moves us at all.

questions.prompt: How does empathy 'distort moral attention'?
options: By directing concern to where help is most needed.
By directing concern to the near and visible few, away from the distant many.

questions.prompt: Why is empathy 'easily enlisted by manipulation'?
options: A vivid case can mobilise feeling for a cause sober judgement would reject.
It is governed by cool reasoning.

questions.prompt: What does the writer conclude empathy must be governed by?
options: Something cooler and more impartial — principles, reasoning and a widened circle.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 共情偏私且不善计数:为眼前个体动情、不为看不见的众生)、细节(B 共情把关切引向近处可见之少数、背离理性的需求分配)、细节(B 生动个案可为冷静判断会拒绝的事业动员情感)、主旨(B 共情须由更冷静公正之原则与推理来统御)。
